QF.id: IV Strategies Using Interest Rate Derivatives_48

question.text: How does leverage work in futures speculation?

answer.text: Traders deposit only margin (a fraction of contract value) but gain exposure to the full contract value, amplifying both gains and losses
Traders can borrow unlimited amounts from the exchange to increase position size
Leverage means the futures price moves more than the spot price
Leverage eliminates the risk of loss while enhancing profit potential

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Futures require only initial margin (typically 5-15% of contract value) but provide exposure to the full notional. A 1% move creates a much larger percentage gain/loss on the margin deployed. **The Trap:** Exchanges don't lend money - margin is a performance guarantee. Futures prices generally track spot closely. Leverage increases BOTH profit and loss. **Cross-Exam Context:** Leverage through margin is a universal futures concept tested in every NISM derivatives exam. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** A Rs. 5 lakh margin on a Rs. 50 lakh contract means 10x leverage. A 2% adverse move wipes out 20% of your margin.
